questions.stem: Why is Python the dominant language for AI and data science despite being slower than C++?
options: Python is actually faster than C++ for mathematical operations
Python's ease of use enables rapid experimentation; numerical bottlenecks are handled by C/CUDA extensions (NumPy, PyTorch) — you get Python's expressiveness with near-C performance for computation
AI researchers exclusively use Python because they don't know C++
Python is the only language that can run on GPUs
questions.explanation: Python is slow for pure Python loops (~100ns per iteration) but this rarely matters: the bottleneck in ML is tensor operations, which run in optimised C/CUDA (NumPy, PyTorch). Python provides the 'glue' for configuration, data loading, training loops, and experiments. The result: fast development cycles with near-optimal runtime for the math. This is why all major ML frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) have Python APIs.

questions.stem: What does the with keyword do in Python (context manager)?
options: Creates a conditional block, like an advanced if statement
Ensures setup and teardown code runs reliably — guarantees __enter__ runs before the block and __exit__ runs after, even if an exception occurs
Creates a new variable scope where variables don't persist after the block
Imports a module temporarily for use within the block
questions.explanation: Context managers use __enter__ and __exit__ to bracket a block. Examples: with open('file') as f: (closes file even if error), with torch.no_grad(): (disables gradient tracking), with torch.autocast('cuda'): (enables mixed precision). The guarantee is that __exit__ ALWAYS runs — preventing resource leaks even when exceptions occur.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of requirements.txt in a Python project?
options: Documents the project's goals and requirements for stakeholders
Lists all Python package dependencies with optional version pins — allows anyone to reproduce the exact environment with pip install -r requirements.txt
Lists the Python files required to run the project
Contains test cases that the code is required to pass
questions.explanation: requirements.txt is a pip-readable file listing dependencies: torch==2.1.0, numpy>=1.24, transformers>=4.35. Running pip install -r requirements.txt installs them all. Without it, collaborators must manually install dependencies or debug version incompatibilities. For reproducibility (essential in ML experiments), always pin exact versions: torch==2.1.0 not just torch.

questions.stem: What is a virtual environment and why should every Python project use one?
options: A virtual machine that runs Python in isolation from the host OS
An isolated directory with its own Python interpreter and packages — prevents version conflicts between projects that need different library versions
A cloud environment for running Python in the browser
A testing environment where code runs without side effects
questions.explanation: Without virtual environments, all Python projects share the same system packages. If Project A needs numpy==1.23 and Project B needs numpy==1.26, only one can be installed. Virtual environments (python -m venv .venv or conda create) create isolated package installations per project. Activate with source .venv/bin/activate; then pip installs go into that environment only.

options: Connects a Python class to a database table automatically
Automatically generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ methods from annotated field definitions — eliminating boilerplate for data-holding classes
Makes a class immutable so its values cannot be changed after creation
Marks a class as a data type that can be used in type hints
questions.explanation: A @dataclass generates common boilerplate automatically. Without it: you write __init__ with every field as a parameter, __repr__ for printing, __eq__ for comparison. With it: annotate fields and Python generates these methods. In ML: use @dataclass for configuration classes (TrainingConfig), data records (Sample), and result objects (EvaluationResult). Add slots=True for 40% less memory per instance.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of torch.manual_seed(42) and np.random.seed(42) at the start of an ML script?
options: They initialise all weights to the value 42
They seed the random number generators so the same 'random' values are produced on every run — making experiments reproducible and bugs debuggable
They set the maximum number of random samples to 42
They are required by PyTorch and NumPy to activate GPU support
questions.explanation: Without seeds, weight initialisation and data shuffling are random — two runs of identical code produce different results, making it impossible to determine if a change improved results or just got lucky. Setting seeds makes random operations deterministic: same seed → same initial weights → same training trajectory → reproducible results. Essential for debugging (reproduce a bug consistently) and reporting (confirm results are reproducible).
